title: Power Perfected in Weakness: Introduction to 2 Corinthians
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: The Theme and Purpose of the Series
subpoints: When we read 2 Corinthians we are not reading ancient correspondence—we are hearing the living Christ address His church
2 Corinthians is among the most personal letters in the New Testament: an apostle opposed, misunderstood, slandered, and wounded by those he loved, yet utterly committed to Christ and the church
The series theme: 'Power Perfected in Weakness'—the thread that runs through the entire letter

2 Corinthians challenges every instinct modern Christians have: we value strength, visibility, charisma, and success; Paul says Christ works most clearly through weakness, suffering, sincerity, and dependence

outline.title: Apostolic Authority: Paul, an Apostle by the Will of God (v. 1a)
subpoints: Paul asserts his apostleship humbly and theologically: 'by the will of God,' not by his own merit, success, or charisma
His authority rests on God's eternal decree—Paul's calling, conversion, sufferings, and conflicts with Corinth were not accidents but governed by God's sovereign purpose
Paul's authority is ministerial, not lordly; he speaks with authority because he speaks Christ's word, not his own
Including Timothy as co-sender models shared, relational, humble leadership—God displays His power through weak servants

outline.title: Spiritual Fatherhood and Shared Ministry: Paul and Timothy (v. 1b)
subpoints: Paul did not merely plant churches—he invested deeply in people, especially men; Timothy is the fruit of long-term discipleship
Ministry is shared: there is no lone ranger in the church of Jesus Christ; Timothy had been sent to Corinth before and knew the people and their struggles
Paul outranks Timothy by every measure, yet calls him 'our brother'—authority in Christ's church never negates fraternity
Christ governs His church through connected, accountable, relational ministry, not isolated authority figures—a deeply Reformed and Presbyterian principle

outline.title: The Nature and Location of the Church (v. 1c)
subpoints: Paul addresses 'the church of God which is at Corinth'—covenantal language asserting the church belongs not to Paul or Corinth but to God
Corinth was an important, wealthy, cosmopolitan city of roughly 80,000 people, morally notorious and religiously pluralistic—in many ways resembling a modern Western city
Paul places the Corinthians within the wider community of Christ's people by addressing 'all the saints who are in all Achaia'—they are part of a regional network, not an isolated outpost
This counters Corinthian spiritual elitism and local pride; the gospel had taken root not only in one city but throughout the entire province

outline.title: The Identity of the Recipients: Saints by Calling, Not Achievement
subpoints: Believers are saints—holy ones—not because of who they are but because of what Christ has done for them
Sainthood flows from union with Christ, not from moral perfection; we are saints by calling, not by achievement
The church has always been scattered yet united, small in the world's eyes yet precious to God, weak in itself yet sustained by grace
No congregation exists independently of Christ's wider body; local churches are accountable beyond themselves

outline.title: Grace and Peace: The Foundation of the Christian Life (v. 2)
subpoints: Grace always precedes peace: grace is God's unmerited favor toward sinners in Christ; peace is the result of receiving that grace
Peace here is not mere emotional calm but covenantal reconciliation through justification by faith alone (Romans 5:1)
The blessing is explicitly Trinitarian: grace and peace from God the Father and the Son, applied by the Spirit
Paul begins with blessing because obedience flows from grace, not from fear—every command in this letter flows from grace already given

outline.title: Six Applications from 2 Corinthians 1:1-2
subpoints: Submit joyfully to Christ's appointed Word—Paul expected the Corinthians to follow what he said, and by implication we should as well
Value ministry in difficult places—God delighted to plant His church in Corinth and still delights to plant churches in hard places; do not abandon hard mission fields
Embrace shared relational leadership—God has appointed elders to rule His church; Paul and Timothy model patient, humble, long-term shared ministry
Love the church despite her flaws—if Christ does not abandon His church, neither should you; being hurt by the church is not grounds for forsaking it
Rest in your identity as saints—you are not defined by your failures but by God's calling in Christ; you are a saint by calling, not achievement
Live from grace, not for grace—every command flows from grace already given; do not wait for more grace to follow Christ, do it now

summary:
This sermon inaugurates a 26-part series through 2 Corinthians under the theme 'Power Perfected in Weakness.' Pastor Tito Lyro confines himself to just the first two verses, using them as the theological foundation for the entire letter and series. He establishes that when we read 2 Corinthians we are not reading ancient correspondence but hearing the living Christ address His church—a church that was real and deeply flawed, representing all of us.

The sermon works through four main elements of the greeting. First, Paul's apostleship: it rests not on his own success or charisma but on the will of God, making it both authoritative and humble. Second, the partnership with Timothy: their relationship models spiritual fatherhood, shared ministry, and ministerial humility. Paul outranks Timothy by every measure yet calls him 'our brother,' illustrating that authority in Christ's church never negates fraternity. Third, the identity of the church: addressed as 'the church of God in Corinth with all the saints in all Achaia,' the Corinthians are placed within a regional network of churches and reminded they belong not to themselves but to God. The saints in Corinth were not alone—and neither are we. Fourth, the greeting of grace and peace: not a mere formality but a theological doorway asserting that grace always precedes peace, that peace is covenantal reconciliation through justification by faith, and that God is responsible for salvation from beginning to end.

Pastor Lyro closes with six applications: submit to Christ's appointed Word; value ministry in difficult places; embrace shared relational leadership; love the church despite her flaws; rest in your identity as saints by calling; and live from grace already given rather than striving to earn more grace.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: 2 Corinthians 1:1-11

Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God, and Timothy our brother, to the church of God which is at Corinth, with all the saints who are in all Achaia. Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ. Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies and God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our tribulation, that we may be able to comfort those who are in any trouble with the comfort with which we ourselves are comforted by God. For as the sufferings of Christ abound in us, so our consolation also abounds through Christ. Now if we are afflicted, it is for your consolation and salvation, which is effective for enduring the same sufferings which we also suffer. Or if we are comforted, it is for your consolation and salvation. And our hope for you is steadfast, because we know that as you are partakers of the sufferings, so also you will be partakers of the consolation. For we do not want you to be ignorant, brethren, of our trouble which came to us in Asia: that we were burdened beyond measure, above strength, so that we despaired even of life. Yes, we had the sentence of death in ourselves, that we should not trust in ourselves but in God who raises the dead, who delivered us from so great a death, and does deliver us; in whom we trust that He will still deliver us, you also helping together in prayer for us, that thanks may be given by many persons on our behalf for the gift granted to us through many.

## Sermon

### Introduction: What Kind of Letter Is This?

When we open the Scriptures and read a letter like this one, we are not simply reading ancient correspondence. This is a real letter that was sent to a real group of people by a real man named Paul—but when we open and read it, we are hearing the living Christ address His church. The Corinthian church was a real church, but it also represents a church representing all of us.

This is especially true in a letter like 2 Corinthians, where theology is forged in suffering and pastoral ministry unfolds in weakness. 2 Corinthians is among the most personal letters in the New Testament. In it we meet an apostle who has been opposed, misunderstood, slandered, and deeply wounded by people whom he loved—and who remains utterly committed to Christ and to Christ's church. This letter teaches us what faithful ministry looks like when obedience is costly and appearances are deceiving.

### The Theme and Structure of This Series

We are starting today a 26-part series through this letter—as firm as the laws of the Medes and the Persians. The theme: Power Perfected in Weakness, because that is the theme of 2 Corinthians.

The goal of this series is not merely to study Paul's biography or resolve historical questions, but to shepherd us into a cross-shaped understanding of Christian living, ministry, suffering, and hope under the new covenant. After all, this letter tells us that we are ministers of the new covenant.

2 Corinthians challenges and confronts nearly every instinct we have as modern Christians. We value strength, visibility, being known, efficiency, charisma, success. And Paul says that Christ works most clearly through weakness, suffering, sincerity, and dependence—not the things that we as modern people would rely on.

Over the coming months this letter will recalibrate how we think about spiritual authority, faithful ministry, affliction-sharing comfort, repentance and restoration, generosity and stewardship, perseverance and assurance. Today, we look only at the first two verses.

### Apostolic Authority: Paul, an Apostle by the Will of God (v. 1a)

Paul begins by asserting his apostleship—but notice that he does so humbly and theologically. "Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God." His authority is not self-generated, nor does it rest on charisma, success, or public approval. He doesn't say "Paul, an apostle who planted many churches" or "Paul, an apostle who has had a lot of success." His apostleship rests on God's eternal decree.

This aligns directly with the Westminster Confession chapter 3, paragraph 1, which teaches that God ordains all things freely and unchangeably. Paul's calling, conversion, sufferings, and conflicts with Corinth were not accidents. God's sovereign purpose governed them all.

Paul's authority is ministerial, not lordly. He speaks with authority because he speaks Christ's word, not his own. What we are considering here is Christ's word to His church.

### Spiritual Fatherhood and Shared Ministry: Paul and Timothy (v. 1b)

Notice that Paul does not stand alone. He says "and Timothy our brother." This is not mere formality. Timothy was Paul's true son and brother in the faith, converted under Paul's ministry and shaped through years of close discipleship.

Though Paul held apostolic office, he calls Timothy "our brother"—reminding us that leadership in Christ's church is relational, humble, and shared. There is no king other than Jesus in the Church of Jesus Christ. There is no single apostle today. God displays His power through weak servants.

Paul and Timothy's relationship shows us at least three important truths: spiritual fatherhood (Paul invested deeply in people, particularly in men, and Timothy is the fruit of long-term discipleship); shared ministry (there is no lone ranger in the church of Jesus Christ; Timothy had been sent to Corinth before); and ministerial humility (Paul outranks Timothy by every measure, yet calls him "our brother," teaching us that authority in Christ's church never negates fraternity).

This reflects a deeply held Reformed and Presbyterian principle: Christ governs His church through connected, accountable, relational ministry, not isolated authority figures.

### The Nature and Location of the Church (v. 1c)

Paul writes to "the church of God which is at Corinth, with all the saints who are in all Achaia." Notice: he doesn't say "to you Corinthians." He says "the church of God in Corinth." This is covenantal language. The church does not belong to Paul. The church does not belong to Corinth. It belongs to God.

Corinth was one of the most important cities in the first-century Roman Empire—about 80,000 people, situated on a narrow isthmus controlling major trade routes, wealthy, cosmopolitan, and morally notorious. If you wanted to insult somebody, you'd call them "a Corinthian." In many ways Corinth resembles the modern Western city: prosperous, ambitious, sophisticated, and at the same time deeply broken.

By addressing the saints throughout Achaia, Paul reminds the Corinthians that they are part of a regional network of churches, not an isolated spiritual outpost. This matters because Corinth tended toward spiritual elitism and local pride—and Paul quietly but firmly re-orients them. They belong to something far bigger than themselves.

There is also a movement today that says only the city matters. But Achaia included rural areas and small churches as well as Corinth. The great Spurgeon was converted in a small rural dissenting chapel of fifteen people by a laymen filling in for an absent pastor. Not only the city matters—everywhere matters. Christ has one church across many places.

### The Identity of the Recipients: Saints by Calling (v. 1c)

Paul addresses all these people—wives, husbands, Corinthians, Achaians—as saints. Look at the person to your right and to your left: if you are believing in Jesus Christ, you are a saint. Sainthood has nothing to do with who you are but everything to do with what Christ has done for you. We are saints by calling, not by achievement.

The church has always been scattered yet united. Always small in the world's eyes, yet precious to God. Always weak in itself, yet sustained by grace. The citizens of Ephesus brought charges against Christians, calling them weak peasants—the lowest of society. These are the people who are turning the world upside down. Can that be said of us today?

### Grace and Peace: The Foundation of Everything (v. 2)

Grace always precedes peace. Grace is God's unmerited favor toward sinners in Christ. Peace is the result of finding the grace of God—reconciliation with God. As Paul says elsewhere in Romans 5: "Therefore, having been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ."

This peace is not mere emotional calm but covenantal reconciliation through justification by faith alone. And notice how this blessing is explicitly Trinitarian: grace and peace from God the Father and the Son, applied by the Spirit.

Paul begins with blessing because obedience flows from grace, not from fear. Every command in this letter flows from grace already given.

### Six Applications

If these truths shaped Paul's relationship with Corinth, they must shape how we think, live, and serve as Christ's church today.

First, submit joyfully to Christ's appointed Word. Paul expected the Corinthians to follow what he said. By implication, that applies to us as well.

Second, value ministry in difficult places. It is very hard in Corinth. God delighted to plant His church there, and He still delights to plant churches in hard places. Don't leave just because it is hard.

Third, embrace shared relational leadership. God has appointed elders to rule His church, not a tyrant. Paul and Timothy model patient, humble, long-term ministry in shared relational leadership.

Fourth, love the church despite her flaws. Paul loved the church of God in Corinth—and reading 1 Corinthians shows what kind of church that was. If Christ does not abandon His church, neither should you.

Fifth, rest in your identity as saints. You are not defined by your failures but by God's calling in Christ. You are a saint by calling, not by achievement.

Sixth, live from grace, not for grace. Do you see the difference? Don't think that if you do these things you get more grace from God—as if you could merit His favor. Every command in this letter flows from grace already given. God already gave you the grace to follow Him. So do it now.

Let us lift our eyes from ourselves to the gracious God who speaks peace to His church. 2 Corinthians opens not with triumph but with grace, not with strength but with calling, not with perfection but with covenant faithfulness. May we learn to see Christ's power perfected in weakness, His grace sufficient for every trial, and His peace guarding His church throughout the world, including here as we follow Him.
